primary_diagnosis.name: Acne vulgaris with acute excoriation and behavioural distress

pathophysiology_summary: Inflammatory acne vulgaris (seborrhoea with open and closed comedones, papules and pustules across the face and upper trunk) has acutely flared, producing intense pruritus. In a patient with an intellectual disability this has driven a compulsive itch-scratch/skin-picking cycle, causing excoriation of the inflamed lesions with pinpoint bleeding and serosanguinous weeping, plus behavioural agitation. The broken epidermal barrier over inflamed, colonised skin creates a real risk of secondary bacterial infection (impetiginisation, and potentially cellulitis), but at presentation the patient is afebrile and systemically well with no spreading erythema, induration, fluctuance or lymphangitic streaking — so infection is a risk to safety-net for, not an established diagnosis. The correct telehealth action is carer-directed wound care (gentle cleansing, protective barrier dressings), pruritus control to break the itch-scratch cycle, protective measures against further self-excoriation, and routine GP follow-up for the underlying acne, with clear escalation triggers for secondary infection, systemic features and unmanageable behavioural escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Itchy, sore, excoriated spots with blackheads, whiteheads and pus-topped lesions on an oily face and upper trunk in an adult — classic inflammatory acne with secondary excoriation.
Raw, bleeding, weeping excoriations breach the skin barrier and can become secondarily infected — must be actively screened for and safety-netted.
Weeping/crusting broken skin could suggest impetigo — reasonable to consider until the origin and appearance of the lesions are clarified.
Painful, eroded, weeping skin in a very distressed patient warrants excluding eczema herpeticum, although there is no eczema history and no systemic illness.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, excoriated acne should lead, but the AI must actively flag secondary infection as the important-not-to-miss risk and screen for it rather than dismiss the presentation as trivial.

differential.evidence_basis: Lesions arise from underlying comedones; patient afebrile and systemically well; no spreading infection — excoriated inflammatory acne confirmed clinically.

No current infection signs (afebrile, no spreading erythema/warmth/swelling, no purulence beyond acne pustules, no lymphangitis) — excluded at present but remains the principal safety-net risk given broken, bleeding skin.
No honey-coloured crusts; the lesions clearly originate from underlying comedones rather than primary superficial infection.
No clustered, monomorphic, punched-out erosions; afebrile with no systemic illness — eczema herpeticum excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: With a negative infection and systemic screen the differential should collapse to excoriated acne. The AI should NOT over-escalate on the basis of the alarming presentation once the red-flag screen is negative.

differential.evidence_basis: Excoriated inflammatory acne with acute behavioural distress in a carer-supported adult; systemically well — carer-directed remote management with routine GP follow-up is appropriate.
Not present now, but the key deterioration risk to safety-net for given broken, bleeding, weeping skin.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final position: excoriated acne, remotely manageable via the carer, with prominent secondary-infection and behavioural safety-netting. Over-triage to ED/000 is the scored defect.

red_flags.description: Spreading redness, warmth or swelling around the excoriated lesions (early impetiginisation / cellulitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate a spreading soft-tissue infection needing in-person assessment and likely oral antibiotics — the principal complication of excoriated, barrier-breached skin.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Evolving cellulitis is missed in a patient who cannot self-monitor, progressing untreated between contacts.

red_flags.description: Fever, systemic unwellness, or lymphangitic streaking tracking from the lesions
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic features or lymphangitis signal cellulitis with systemic involvement requiring ED-level assessment and IV antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Systemic infection progresses to sepsis if not recognised and escalated.

red_flags.description: Escalating behavioural agitation with ongoing self-excoriation / self-harm in a vulnerable adult
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ongoing self-inflicted skin trauma and a safety risk; currently carer-managed but can escalate beyond what support staff can safely contain.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Uncontrolled self-harm, worsening skin trauma, or a behavioural crisis that endangers the patient or others.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Visceral bias / over-triage of a vulnerable patient
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The alarming picture (bleeding, agitation, an intellectually disabled adult, an after-hours call) provokes reflexive over-escalation to ED/000, when the red-flag screen is negative and carer-directed wound care plus itch relief plus routine GP follow-up is the correct, proportionate response.

Attributing the picking to a primary psychiatric or delusional cause (e.g. delusional parasitosis) and missing the genuine inflammatory dermatosis that is driving the pruritus and the picking behaviour.
Anchoring on 'just acne' and stopping, failing to actively screen for and safety-net against impetiginisation/cellulitis in excoriated, bleeding skin.
Reaching for systemic antibiotics despite no established infection (afebrile, no spreading erythema, no purulence beyond acne pustules, no lymphangitis).

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct palpation for warmth, induration, fluctuance or a deep abscess
Whether early secondary bacterial infection is present within the excoriated lesions
Accurate temperature and vital signs without a device
Lymphangitic streaking or regional lymphadenopathy on examination
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage remotely by directing the support worker in wound care (gentle saline/cooled-boiled-water cleansing, hydrocolloid or clean non-stick barrier dressings), an OTC oral antihistamine for pruritus, and protective measures (soft cotton gloves at night, avoid picking), with routine GP review in 3–5 days and clear infection/self-harm safety-netting. In-person urgent review only if infection signs, systemic features, or unmanageable behavioural escalation develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided: (1) dismissing the excoriated, bleeding skin as trivial and failing to safety-net for secondary infection; (2) over-escalating an afebrile, systemically-well patient to ED/000 when carer-supported home care with routine follow-up is appropriate.
